source_paper_title: Allocating Indivisible Goods to Strategic Agents: Pure Nash Equilibria and Fairness
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Amanatidis_2024, title={Allocating Indivisible Goods to Strategic Agents: Pure Nash Equilibria and Fairness}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0058}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0058}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Amanatidis, Georgios and Birmpas, Georgios and Fusco, Federico and Lazos, Philip and Leonardi, Stefano and Reiffenhäuser, Rebecca}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2425–2445} }
context:
Page 22, Section 5 (Discussion)

Theorems 4.5 and 4.7 leave an open question on the MMS guarantee that the equilibria of mechanisms that always have PNE and these are EFX. Although we suspect that the corresponding allocations are not always MMS, such a result would immediately imply that for every such mechanism which runs in polynomial time, finding a best response of an agent is a computationally hard problem.
title: Determine the MMS guarantee of EFX pure Nash equilibria for two agents
background_latex:
Consider a finite set of indivisible goods \(M\) and two agents \(N=\{1,2\}\). Each agent \(i\) has a nonnegative additive valuation \(v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), i.e., \(v_i(S)=\sum_{g\in S} v_i(g)\) for all \(S\subseteq M\).

An allocation is a partition \(A=(A_1,A_2)\) of \(M\). For \(\alpha\in(0,1]\), the allocation is \(\alpha\)-maximin-share fair (\(\alpha\)-MMS) for agent \(i\) if \(v_i(A_i)\ge \alpha\,\mu_i\), where the maximin share is
\[\mu_i = \mu_i(2,M) := \max_{(P_1,P_2)\in \Pi_2(M)} \min\{v_i(P_1),v_i(P_2)\},\]
with \(\Pi_2(M)\) the set of all 2-way partitions of \(M\).

Envy-freeness up to any good (EFX) for two agents means: for each \(i\ne j\), for every good \(g\in A_j\) with \(v_i(g)>0\),
\[ v_i(A_i) \ge v_i(A_j\setminus\{g\}). \]

A (deterministic, no-money) allocation mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) maps a bid profile \(b=(b_1,b_2)\), where each \(b_i\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^{|M|}\), to an allocation \(\mathcal{M}(b)\). Given true valuations \(v=(v_1,v_2)\), a bid profile \(b\) is a pure Nash equilibrium (PNE) if no agent can increase her true utility \(v_i\big(\mathcal{M}(b)_i\big)\) by unilaterally changing her bid vector.

Say that a mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) has EFX PNE on all instances if for every pair \((v_1,v_2)\) it admits at least one PNE, and for every PNE \(b\), the induced allocation \(\mathcal{M}(b)\) is EFX with respect to the true valuations \((v_1,v_2)\).
problem_definition_latex:
Find the best universal constant \(\alpha^*\in[0,1]\) such that the following holds:

For every mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) that, for every additive-valuation instance \((v_1,v_2)\), has at least one pure Nash equilibrium and every pure Nash equilibrium induces an EFX allocation with respect to \((v_1,v_2)\), every such equilibrium allocation \(A=\mathcal{M}(b)\) satisfies
\[ v_i(A_i) \ge \alpha^*\,\mu_i \quad \text{for both } i\in\{1,2\}. \]

Equivalently, determine the tight worst-case MMS approximation guarantee (as a function-free constant) enjoyed by EFX allocations arising at PNE of mechanisms whose PNE always exist and are always EFX. In particular, decide whether one can always take \(\alpha^*=1\) (MMS always), or whether \(\alpha^*<1\), and if so, determine the optimal \(\alpha^*\) (the paper proves existence of some \(\alpha>2/3\)).
latex:
Consider a finite set of indivisible goods \(M\) and two agents \(N=\{1,2\}\). Each agent \(i\) has a nonnegative additive valuation \(v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), i.e., \(v_i(S)=\sum_{g\in S} v_i(g)\) for all \(S\subseteq M\).

\[\mu_i = \mu_i(2,M) := \max_{(P_1,P_2)\in \Pi_2(M)} \min\{v_i(P_1),v_i(P_2)\},\]

Given true valuations \(v=(v_1,v_2)\), a bid profile \(b\) is a pure Nash equilibrium (PNE) if no agent can increase her true utility \(v_i\big(\mathcal{M}(b)_i\big)\) by unilaterally changing her bid vector.

Find the best universal constant \(\alpha^*\in[0,1]\) such that the following holds:

For every mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) that, for every additive-valuation instance \((v_1,v_2)\), has at least one pure Nash equilibrium and every pure Nash equilibrium induces an EFX allocation with respect to \((v_1,v_2)\), every such equilibrium allocation \(A=\mathcal{M}(b)\) satisfies

open_problem_quote: Theorems 4.5 and 4.7 leave an open question on the MMS guarantee that the equilibria of mechanisms that always have PNE and these are EFX. Although we suspect that the corresponding allocations are not always MMS, such a result would immediately imply that for every such mechanism which runs in polynomial time, finding a best response of an agent is a computationally hard problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For a fixed candidate mechanism, one can search over small additive instances and check (by enumeration of bid deviations on discretized grids) whether some PNE outcome violates a target MMS ratio, but quantifying over all mechanisms makes the search indirect. Still, automated searches can be informative by testing natural mechanism families or by attempting to construct valuation profiles that force low-MMS EFX equilibria.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could parameterize a family of mechanisms (e.g., cut-and-choose style or sequential/draft rules) and use a fitness signal based on worst-case equilibrium MMS ratio found by an inner solver that searches for PNE and evaluates EFX and MMS. The nested equilibrium search is computationally heavy and may be noisy/incomplete, but could plausibly produce explicit candidate mechanisms or instances that improve bounds or refute conjectured \(\alpha^*=1\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for the tight universal constant \(\alpha^*\) such that, for every two-agent additive instance and every mechanism whose pure Nash equilibria (PNE) always exist and whose every PNE outcome is EFX with respect to the true valuations, every equilibrium allocation is \(\alpha^*\)-MMS for both agents. In the non-strategic setting, EFX alone only implies \(2/3\)-MMS and this is tight (Theorem 2.6 in the source paper, via Amanatidis et al. 2018). The source paper shows that imposing the strategic equilibrium requirement strengthens the MMS guarantee beyond what EFX alone provides.

Concretely, the source paper proves three key partial results. First, for the specific Plaut--Roughgarden Mod-Cut&Choose mechanism, PNE always exist and every PNE outcome is MMS (hence EFX for \(n=2\)) (Theorem 4.3). Second, for the entire class of mechanisms with guaranteed PNE existence and universal EFX-at-PNE, when \(|M|=4\) every PNE outcome must be MMS (Theorem 4.5), already separating equilibrium EFX from arbitrary EFX allocations. Third, for general \(|M|\), every such mechanism guarantees \(\alpha\)-MMS at every PNE for some universal constant \(\alpha>2/3\) (Theorem 4.7), but the proof is non-quantitative about the best possible \(\alpha\) and does not settle whether \(\alpha^*=1\).

The forward-citing literature provided here does not resolve the tight value of \(\alpha^*\). The two surveys ("Fair division of indivisible goods: Recent progress and open questions" and "Algorithmic fair allocation of indivisible items: A survey and new questions") contextualize the question among EFX/MMS and strategic equilibrium research but do not improve bounds. Related incentive frameworks ("Fair shares: Feasibility, domination, and incentives") and work in different strategic models ("Fair Division with Interdependent Values") indicate alternative ways to obtain MMS-like guarantees under incentives, but they do not address the stringent requirement that every PNE be EFX in the independent additive model. Thus, determining whether \(\alpha^*=1\) or finding the optimal \(\alpha^*<1\) remains open; promising directions include constructing explicit counterexamples (mechanisms/instances) that force an EFX PNE below MMS, or strengthening the structural arguments in Theorem 4.7 to yield an explicit tight constant.

sources.title: Fair division of indivisible goods: Recent progress and open questions
sources.theorem_or_result: This paper is a survey and does not prove new theorems about the maximin-share (MMS) guarantee of EFX pure Nash equilibria (PNE) mechanisms for two agents. The only directly relevant content is a summary of prior work: it cites Amanatidis et al. [2021b] on mechanisms with always-existing PNE whose equilibrium outcomes are EF1, and it poses an open question about whether stronger guarantees than EF1 are possible at PNE (Open Problem 10).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the tight worst-case MMS approximation factor \(\alpha^*\) guaranteed at every PNE of any mechanism whose PNE always exist and are always EFX (for two additive agents). This survey does not analyze EFX-at-PNE mechanisms or derive any MMS lower/upper bounds for such equilibria. It only provides background on EFX and MMS notions and briefly discusses strategic agents, noting that equilibrium analysis has been done for Round-Robin (yielding EF1 at equilibrium) and asking whether stronger-than-EF1 equilibrium guarantees exist. Thus it neither resolves nor makes partial progress on determining \(\alpha^*\).

sources.title: Algorithmic fair allocation of indivisible items: A survey and new questions
sources.theorem_or_result: This paper is a survey; it does not prove a new theorem resolving the open problem. It summarizes known results on EFX, MMS, and strategic behavior in allocation mechanisms, including that for two agents Divide-and-Choose returns an allocation that is MMS and EFX (under appropriate tie-breaking/leximin++), and it cites Amanatidis et al. (2021b) for the result that Round-Robin PNE outcomes are EF1 w.r.t. true values.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the tight worst-case MMS approximation \,\(\alpha^*\) guaranteed by EFX allocations that arise as pure Nash equilibria of mechanisms whose PNE always exist and are always EFX. This survey does not analyze MMS guarantees of EFX-PNE mechanisms, nor does it provide bounds or constructions for \,\(\alpha^*\). It only provides context: (i) relationships between EFX and MMS in non-strategic settings (e.g., Divide-and-Choose for two agents yields MMS+EFX), and (ii) mentions a related strategic-PNE fairness line (PNE of Round-Robin are EF1), but not EFX nor MMS-at-PNE guarantees.

sources.title: Fair shares: Feasibility, domination, and incentives
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper develops a framework of feasible shares and self-maximizing (truthful-in-guarantee) shares for fair division of indivisible goods. For additive valuations it proves (among other results) that there is no SM-dominant feasible share (Theorem 1), and it constructs feasible, self-maximizing, polynomial-time computable shares that approximately dominate MMS: for general n a share that is \(\frac{2n}{3n-1}\)-dominating, for \(n\le 4\) a \(\frac45\)-dominating share, and for two agents a PTAS share that is \((1-\varepsilon)\)-dominating (Theorem 3 / Theorem 5 / Theorem 6).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the tight worst-case MMS approximation \(\alpha^*\) guaranteed by EFX allocations that arise as pure Nash equilibria of mechanisms whose PNE always exist and are always EFX (two additive agents). This paper does not study EFX, mechanisms, or Nash equilibria of allocation mechanisms; instead it studies share guarantees (feasible shares, self-maximization) and approximation to MMS. It only mentions that feasible shares imply equilibrium guarantees in a generic sense (Proposition 1), but it does not analyze EFX-PNE allocations nor derive an \(\alpha^*\) bound for them. Hence it does not resolve the EFX-PNE MMS-guarantee question, though it provides related MMS-approximation tools and context.

sources.title: Fair Division with Interdependent Values
sources.theorem_or_result: Theorem 3.1: For two agents with equal entitlements and general monotone interdependent valuations, there exists a (guessing) mechanism such that for every signal vector there exists a PNE whose outcome is MMS and EFX for one agent (the cutter) and EF for the other (the chooser) w.r.t. the true signals. Additionally, Lemma 3.4 shows in that equilibrium the cutter gets at least her MMS and the allocation is EFX for her.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the tight worst-case MMS approximation guarantee \(\alpha^*\) enjoyed by EFX allocations that arise at PNE of mechanisms where (i) a PNE always exists and (ii) every PNE outcome is EFX (w.r.t. true additive values) for two agents. This paper does not study that guarantee. Its main positive result for \(n=2\) gives existence of at least one fair PNE (not that all PNE are fair), and the equilibrium outcome is only partially EFX (EFX for the cutter, merely EF for the chooser). Moreover, the setting is interdependent values and general monotone valuations, not the independent additive setting of the open problem. The paper’s negative results (Theorem 6.1) show impossibility of mechanisms where all equilibria are MMS/EF1-fair in the interdependent model, but do not address mechanisms with all equilibria EFX nor the MMS level of EFX equilibria in the independent additive model.

sources.title: On Truthful Mechanisms without Pareto-efficiency: Characterizations and Fairness
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves a characterization: for any permutations-closed class of strict monotone preferences (including strict additive valuations), a deterministic mechanism that is strategy-proof (truthful in dominant strategies), non-bossy, and neutral is a serial-quota mechanism (agents pick up to fixed quotas in a fixed order), even without Pareto-efficiency and even allowing disposal of goods (Theorem 1 / Application 1).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the MMS guarantee of EFX allocations that arise at pure Nash equilibria of mechanisms whose PNE always exist and are always EFX (two agents, additive valuations). This paper studies a different incentive notion (dominant-strategy truthfulness) and different fairness targets (MMS and EF1), and it does not analyze EFX or pure Nash equilibria. Hence it does not determine the constant \(\alpha^*\) for EFX-PNE mechanisms. Indirectly, it provides structural tools (serial-quota characterization under truthfulness+non-bossy+neutral) and shows strong MMS impossibilities for such truthful mechanisms, which may inform comparisons with equilibrium-based (PNE) approaches.
